text: Apollo 11 was the American spaceflight that first landed humans on the Moon.
Commander Neil Armstrong and lunar module pilot Buzz Aldrin landed the Apollo Lunar Module Eagle on July 20, 1969, at 20:17 UTC.
Armstrong became the first person to step onto the lunar surface six hours and 39 minutes later on July 21 at 02:56 UTC.
Aldrin joined him 19 minutes later.
They spent about two and a quarter hours together exploring the site they had named Tranquility Base upon landing.
Armstrong and Aldrin collected 47.5 pounds of lunar material to bring back to Earth.
Command module pilot Michael Collins flew the command module Columbia alone in lunar orbit while they were on the Moon's surface.
Armstrong and Aldrin spent 21 hours and 36 minutes on the lunar surface at a site they named Tranquility Base before lifting off to rejoin Columbia in lunar orbit.
Apollo 11 was launched by a Saturn V rocket from Kennedy Space Center on Merritt Island, Florida, on July 16 at 13:32 UTC.
It was the fifth crewed mission of NASA's Apollo program.

The Apollo spacecraft had three parts: a command module with a cabin for the three astronauts, the only part that returned to Earth; a service module, which supported the command module with propulsion, electrical power, oxygen, and water; and a lunar module that had two stages—a descent stage for landing on the Moon and an ascent stage to place the astronauts back into lunar orbit.
After being sent to the Moon by the Saturn V's third stage, the astronauts separated the spacecraft from it and traveled for three days until they entered lunar orbit.
Armstrong and Aldrin then moved into Eagle and landed in the Sea of Tranquility on July 20.
The astronauts used Eagle's ascent stage to lift off from the lunar surface and rejoin Collins in the command module.
They jettisoned Eagle before they performed the maneuvers that propelled Columbia out of the last of its 30 lunar orbits onto a trajectory back to Earth.
They returned to Earth and splashed down in the Pacific Ocean on July 24 after more than eight days in space.
Armstrong's first step onto the lunar surface was broadcast on live television to a worldwide audience.
He described the event as 'one small step for a man, one giant leap for mankind.'
Apollo 11 effectively proved that it was possible to land humans on the Moon and return them safely to Earth, fulfilling President John F. Kennedy's goal of reaching the Moon by the end of the 1960s.
President Richard Nixon spoke with Armstrong and Aldrin via a telephone-radio transmission while they walked on the lunar surface.
He congratulated them and said the call was 'the most historic telephone call ever made from the White House.'
A plaque left on the ladder of Eagle's descent stage read: 'Here men from the planet Earth first set foot upon the Moon, July 1969 A.D. We came in peace for all mankind.'
Nixon greeted the astronauts on their return aboard the USS Hornet.
After the crew splashed down, they were quarantined for 21 days as a precaution against Moon germs.
The three astronauts were awarded the Presidential Medal of Freedom by President Nixon.
In 2009, the U.S. House of Representatives passed a resolution recognizing the 40th anniversary of the Apollo 11 flight and its crew members.
Neil Armstrong was born on August 5, 1930, in Wapakoneta, Ohio.
He was a naval aviator who flew combat missions during the Korean War before becoming a test pilot at the National Advisory Committee for Aeronautics High-Speed Flight Station.
He joined the NASA Astronaut Corps in 1962 and served as command pilot of Gemini 8 in 1966, becoming the first civilian to fly a U.S. spacecraft.
As spacecraft commander of Apollo 11, he earned the distinction of being the first person to walk on the Moon.
After the mission, he resigned from NASA in 1971 and taught aerospace engineering at the University of Cincinnati until 1979.
He avoided publicity and rarely gave interviews about Apollo 11 in his later years.
Armstrong died on August 25, 2012, at the age of 82, following complications from cardiovascular surgery.
Buzz Aldrin was born Edwin Eugene Aldrin Jr. on January 20, 1930, in Montclair, New Jersey.
He graduated from the United States Military Academy at West Point in 1951 and earned a Doctor of Science degree in astronautics from the Massachusetts Institute of Technology.
His doctoral thesis on orbital rendezvous techniques contributed directly to NASA's docking procedures.
Aldrin was selected as part of NASA's third group of astronauts in 1963.
He performed the last mission of the Gemini program, Gemini 12, during which he spent over five hours on extravehicular activity.
As lunar module pilot on Apollo 11, he was the second person to walk on the Moon.
After the mission, he became an advocate for human exploration of Mars.
He developed a concept called the Aldrin cycler, a spacecraft trajectory that would make regular trips between Earth and Mars.
Michael Collins was born on October 31, 1930, in Rome, Italy, where his father was serving as a U.S. military attaché.
He graduated from West Point in 1952 and became an experimental test pilot at Edwards Air Force Base.
Collins was selected as part of NASA's third group of astronauts in 1963, along with Aldrin.
He flew on Gemini 10 in 1966, during which he performed two extravehicular activities.
On Apollo 11, he remained alone in lunar orbit aboard Columbia while Armstrong and Aldrin explored the surface below.
He later wrote that he felt no loneliness during the 48 minutes of each orbit when he was out of radio contact with Earth.
After the Apollo 11 mission, Collins left NASA and became the first director of the National Air and Space Museum in Washington, D.C.
He served in that role from 1971 to 1978, overseeing the museum's opening in its current building on the National Mall in 1976.
Collins died on April 28, 2021, at the age of 90, after a battle with cancer.
The Saturn V rocket that launched Apollo 11 was designated SA-506 and was the sixth Saturn V to fly.
It stood 363 feet tall and weighed 6.5 million pounds when fully fueled.
The rocket's first stage, the S-IC, was powered by five F-1 engines that together produced 7.5 million pounds of thrust.
These engines burned a mixture of liquid oxygen and RP-1 kerosene for approximately 2 minutes and 41 seconds.
The second stage, the S-II, used five J-2 engines burning liquid hydrogen and liquid oxygen.
It provided thrust for approximately 6 minutes, accelerating the spacecraft to nearly orbital velocity.
The third stage, the S-IVB, had a single J-2 engine that fired twice—once to achieve Earth orbit and again to send the spacecraft toward the Moon in a maneuver called translunar injection.
The launch took place at Launch Complex 39A at Kennedy Space Center, with an estimated one million spectators watching from the surrounding highways and beaches.
Vice President Spiro Agnew and former President Lyndon B. Johnson were among the dignitaries who attended the launch in person.
The launch vehicle performed flawlessly, placing Apollo 11 into its translunar trajectory just 12 minutes after liftoff.
The lunar module Eagle was designed and built by Grumman Aircraft Engineering Corporation.
It consisted of two stages: a descent stage with a throttleable engine for landing, and an ascent stage that served as the crew cabin and contained a fixed-thrust engine for returning to orbit.
Eagle's descent engine could be throttled from 10% to full thrust, allowing Armstrong to control the rate of descent during landing.
During the powered descent, program alarms sounded in the cockpit—the famous 1202 and 1201 alarms indicating the computer was being overloaded with tasks.
Flight controller Steve Bales in Mission Control determined the alarms were acceptable and called 'Go' for the landing to continue.
Armstrong took semi-automatic control of the lunar module in the final minutes of descent, steering it past a boulder-strewn crater to find a safe landing spot.
When Eagle landed, only about 25 seconds of fuel remained in the descent stage.
Armstrong radioed 'Houston, Tranquility Base here. The Eagle has landed,' to which Charles Duke, the capsule communicator, replied, 'Roger, Tranquility, we copy you on the ground.'
The landing site was in the southern Sea of Tranquility, about 20 kilometers southwest of the crater Sabine D.
Armstrong described the surface as covered with fine-grained material resembling powdered charcoal.

The astronauts deployed several scientific instruments on the lunar surface, including a passive seismic experiment to measure moonquakes and a laser ranging retroreflector that is still in use today.
They also deployed a solar wind composition experiment, which consisted of a sheet of aluminum foil exposed to the solar wind for approximately 77 minutes.
Armstrong and Aldrin planted an American flag on the lunar surface, though the flag fell over when Eagle's ascent stage lifted off.
The astronauts left behind medallions bearing the names of the three Apollo 1 astronauts who died in the 1967 launch pad fire, and two cosmonauts who were also deceased.
Upon their return to Earth, the Apollo 11 crew was placed in the Mobile Quarantine Facility, a modified Airstream trailer aboard the USS Hornet.
The quarantine lasted 21 days and was intended to prevent the potential spread of lunar pathogens to Earth's biosphere.
Scientists at the Lunar Receiving Laboratory in Houston examined the lunar samples and found no evidence of living organisms.
The quarantine requirement was dropped after Apollo 14 when it became clear that the Moon was lifeless.
The lunar samples brought back by Apollo 11 were found to be basaltic rocks between 3.1 and 4.4 billion years old.

Analysis of these samples fundamentally changed scientific understanding of the Moon's origin, supporting the giant-impact hypothesis that the Moon formed from debris after a Mars-sized body collided with Earth.
The mission's success was broadcast to an estimated 600 million television viewers worldwide, making it one of the most watched events in human history.
The broadcast used a specially designed slow-scan television camera mounted on the equipment bay of Eagle's descent stage.
The camera was deployed by Armstrong as he descended the ladder, providing live footage of his first steps.
The television signal was received by tracking stations in Australia—the Honeysuckle Creek station and the Parkes Radio Observatory—and relayed to Houston.
After completing their lunar surface activities, Armstrong and Aldrin returned to the lunar module and sealed the hatch.
They rested for several hours before beginning the launch preparations for Eagle's ascent stage.
The ascent engine fired successfully, lifting Eagle off the lunar surface and into orbit to rendezvous with Columbia.
Collins performed the docking maneuver, connecting Eagle to the command module's forward hatch.
After the crew transferred back to Columbia, Eagle was jettisoned and its orbit eventually decayed, causing it to crash somewhere on the lunar surface.
Columbia's service propulsion system engine then fired to place the spacecraft on its return trajectory to Earth.
The return trip took approximately 60 hours.
Columbia re-entered Earth's atmosphere on July 24, 1969, at a speed of approximately 24,000 miles per hour.
The command module's heat shield protected the crew from temperatures exceeding 5,000 degrees Fahrenheit during re-entry.
Three parachutes deployed to slow Columbia's descent, and it splashed down in the Pacific Ocean approximately 900 miles southwest of Hawaii at 16:50 UTC.
The Apollo 11 mission lasted a total of 8 days, 3 hours, 18 minutes, and 35 seconds from launch to splashdown.
Following their quarantine, the three astronauts embarked on a 38-day world tour called the 'Giant Leap' tour, visiting 24 countries.
They were celebrated with ticker-tape parades in New York, Chicago, and Los Angeles.
The legacy of Apollo 11 extends far beyond the technical achievement; it demonstrated what humanity could accomplish through determination and international cooperation.
The mission inspired generations of scientists, engineers, and explorers, and remains a defining moment of the 20th century.
The command module Columbia is on display at the National Air and Space Museum in Washington, D.C., where it has been seen by millions of visitors since 1976.
